worth_it_explanation: The Trade Desk's measurement marketplace connects campaigns to third-party partners for brand lift, in-store visits, reach, and frequency measurement. Without these integrations, campaigns measured by awareness or store-visit goals have no way to confirm whether those goals are being met. Clicks and impression counts do not answer those questions. The checks here are configuration reviews inside the The Trade Desk UI, not technical rebuilds, and they close gaps that would otherwise let an entire awareness campaign report no meaningful outcome.
intro: The Trade Desk's measurement marketplace lets advertisers connect to third-party measurement vendors, such as brand lift survey providers, in-store visitation firms, and reach-and-frequency measurement panels, directly from within a campaign. These integrations replace impression counts as the proof-of-performance for brand and awareness objectives. These checks confirm that the right measurement partners are attached to the relevant campaigns, that reach and frequency are counted by people rather than raw impressions, and that third-party partner numbers are reconciled against The Trade Desk's own platform reporting where both are available.

audit_checks.title: Campaigns with awareness or in-store goals have no third-party measurement partner attached
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A campaign optimizing toward brand awareness or store visits has no way to confirm the goal was met if no measurement partner is attached. Clicks and impression counts do not answer those questions. The campaign runs, the budget is spent, and the outcome remains unverified.

educate.base:
The Trade Desk's measurement marketplace is a directory of third-party measurement vendors integrated directly into the platform. Advertisers connect a measurement partner to a campaign so that partner can track outcomes the campaign was designed to produce: whether aided brand awareness shifted, whether a person who saw an ad later visited a store, or whether a target audience was reached without excess frequency.

When a campaign targets an awareness or in-store objective but no partner is attached, the only numbers available after the flight are impressions, reach estimates from the platform, and any clicks. None of those numbers answer whether the intended outcome occurred. A brand running a connected TV (CTV) awareness campaign without a brand lift study attached cannot tell whether brand recall or purchase intent moved. A retailer running a display campaign without an in-store visit partner attached cannot tell whether foot traffic responded.

Setting up a measurement partner in The Trade Desk takes place at the campaign level. The measurement marketplace lists available partners by measurement type, including brand lift, in-store visits, foot traffic attribution, and reach. You may want to attach a partner before the campaign launches. Some providers, including brand lift survey vendors, require a minimum impression threshold to deliver a statistically valid result, and a partner attached mid-flight may not collect enough data.

A few signs point to this gap:
  • The campaign has an awareness, consideration, or in-store objective but the measurement section shows no partner attached
  • Post-flight reporting references only impressions and click-through rate (CTR) for a campaign where neither metric is the stated goal
  • Past campaigns in the account ran to completion with no third-party outcome report on file
You may want to review all active campaigns against their stated objectives before the next flight launches.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running display or CTV campaigns with a store-visit goal, an in-store attribution partner, such as a location data provider integrated through the measurement marketplace, is the mechanism that connects ad exposure to a physical visit. Without it, the campaign produces impression data but no outcome evidence a client or internal stakeholder can act on.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead-generation advertisers running awareness phases before a direct-response phase may want to attach a brand lift or reach partner to the upper-funnel campaign to confirm that awareness is building. Relying on the direct-response campaign's conversion data alone does not capture whether the awareness spend contributed.

investigate.intro: Open each active or recently completed campaign and confirm whether a third-party measurement partner is attached. Focus on campaigns whose stated goal is awareness, consideration, or in-store visits.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: In The Trade Desk UI, open the campaign you want to review.
Navigate to the campaign-level settings and find the Measurement section or the Insights & Measurement tab.
Note whether a measurement partner is listed. Record the partner name and measurement type if one is present, or note 'none attached' if the section is empty.
Cross-reference the campaign's objective. If the objective is brand awareness, consideration, or in-store visits and no partner is attached, flag the campaign.
Repeat for each campaign with a non-click objective in the account.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each campaign with an awareness or in-store goal, whether a third-party measurement partner is attached and whether the partner type matches the campaign objective. A campaign with a relevant partner attached and active passes. A campaign with no partner attached, or a partner type that does not match the objective, is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the missing or mismatched partner type.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the measurement partner status for each campaign with an awareness or in-store objective above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more campaigns on {{site_name}} are running with awareness or in-store goals but have no third-party measurement partner attached through The Trade Desk's measurement marketplace. Without a brand lift, in-store visit, or reach partner collecting data during the flight, there is no outcome evidence beyond impressions to confirm whether the campaign's goal was met. {{voice_recommend}} identifying the appropriate measurement partner for each affected campaign in the marketplace, attaching it before the next flight launches, and confirming the partner has collected enough impressions to deliver a statistically valid report at the end of the flight.

audit_checks.title: Reach and frequency are evaluated using raw impression counts rather than people-based measurement
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Raw impression counts overstate reach and mask frequency concentration. A campaign that appears to have reached a broad audience may have served most of its impressions to a small group of people at high repetition. Bidding and budget decisions made against inflated reach numbers produce campaigns that under-deliver on breadth and over-deliver on frequency to the same users.

educate.base:
Impression count and reach are not the same number. An impression is a single ad served. Reach counts the distinct people who were served at least one impression. A campaign with ten million impressions could have reached ten million people once each, or it could have reached one million people ten times each. The raw impression count is the same either way. Without people-based reach measurement, the difference is invisible in the reporting.

The Trade Desk supports people-based reach and frequency measurement through Unified ID 2.0 (UID2), its cross-device identity framework, and through third-party measurement partners integrated via the measurement marketplace. UID2 links ad exposures across devices to a pseudonymous identifier derived from a hashed email or phone number, which lets the platform count distinct people rather than distinct cookies or device IDs. Third-party reach partners apply their own panels and modeling to produce deduplicated reach figures, including across publishers where UID2 coverage may vary.

When reach is reported using raw impressions or cookie-based counts alone, the number inflates because the same person can be counted multiple times across devices, browsers, or sessions. The resulting frequency number also deflates for the same reason: a person who saw the ad six times across three devices may appear as three people who each saw it twice.

A few signs point to reach being measured by impressions rather than people:
  • Campaign reporting shows no deduplicated reach metric or references impression reach rather than unique reach
  • No reach or frequency measurement partner is attached and UID2 targeting is not active for the campaign
  • Frequency caps are set but there is no people-based signal to enforce them across devices
You may want to confirm that a people-based signal, either UID2 or a third-party reach partner, is active before treating reach numbers as accurate.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a CTV or cross-channel campaign, the gap between impression reach and people-based reach is largest when the same household sees the ad across a connected TV, a tablet, and a phone. Without a deduplicated signal, the household counts as three separate reached users at low frequency rather than one household at high frequency.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead-generation advertisers running sequential messaging campaigns depend on accurate frequency data to know when to move a user from an awareness creative to a direct-response creative. If frequency is being counted by device rather than by person, the sequencing fires at the wrong stage for users who carry multiple devices.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether reach and frequency in campaign reporting are derived from a people-based signal, such as UID2 or a third-party measurement partner, rather than from raw impression or cookie counts.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the campaign and navigate to its reporting view.
Check whether the reach metric shown is labeled as unique reach, deduplicated reach, or people-based reach, rather than total impressions or impression reach.

Navigate to the campaign's identity settings and confirm whether UID2 targeting is enabled. A campaign using UID2 has a people-based frequency signal across the devices where that identifier is available.
Check the Measurement section to see whether a reach and frequency partner is attached. Note the partner name if present.
If neither UID2 nor a reach partner is active, the reach figure in reporting reflects impression or cookie count, not people count.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each campaign where reach is a reported or stated objective, whether a people-based signal is active: either UID2 targeting enabled, a third-party reach partner attached, or both. A campaign with at least one people-based signal active passes. A campaign where reach is reported using only raw impressions or cookie counts, with neither UID2 nor a reach partner present, is a fail recorded with the campaign name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the reach measurement method for each campaign where reach is a stated or reported objective above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that reach and frequency on {{site_name}}'s campaigns are being evaluated using raw impression counts rather than a people-based signal. Without Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) targeting or a third-party reach partner attached, the same person counted across multiple devices inflates the reported reach figure and deflates the reported frequency. The campaign may appear to be reaching more people at lower frequency than it actually is. {{voice_recommend}} enabling UID2 targeting on relevant campaigns and attaching a people-based reach partner through The Trade Desk's measurement marketplace so that reach reporting counts distinct individuals rather than distinct impressions.

audit_checks.title: Third-party measurement results are not reconciled against The Trade Desk platform reporting
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Discrepancies between a third-party measurement partner's numbers and The Trade Desk's own reporting are normal and expected because each applies different methodology, attribution windows, and deduplication logic. When no reconciliation step takes place, the gap goes unexplained, which leads to conflicting reports being shared with stakeholders without context, and can make valid performance look like a measurement failure.

educate.base:
When a campaign runs with both a third-party measurement partner and The Trade Desk's native reporting active, two sets of numbers exist at the end of the flight. They will not match exactly. Each source uses different counting methods. The platform counts an impression at the time of delivery. A brand lift vendor counts survey responses from exposed users. An in-store visit partner matches device location signals to store visit events within a defined window. A reach partner applies its own panel and modeling. These are different measurements of different things, and the differences are expected.

What is not expected is for those discrepancies to go unexamined. When a client receives a platform report showing fifteen million impressions and a partner report showing four hundred thousand reached households, and no one has explained the relationship between the two numbers, the client may assume one set of numbers is wrong rather than understanding that both are measuring different dimensions of the same campaign.

Reconciliation means documenting, for each measurement source active on a campaign, what that source measures, what its methodology produces, and how its output relates to platform-reported numbers. The goal is not to make the numbers agree. The goal is to confirm that the differences fall within the expected range for the methodology involved, and to note any discrepancy that falls outside that range. An unexpected gap can indicate a tracking or setup error.

A few signs point to reconciliation not taking place:
  • Post-campaign reports from the partner and from The Trade Desk are stored separately with no comparison document
  • Stakeholder communications reference one set of numbers without acknowledging the other
  • Discrepancies larger than the expected methodological variance are present but carry no explanation
You may want to establish a reconciliation step as a standard part of campaign wrap-up for any campaign where both sources are active.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer with both a platform impression report and an in-store visit attribution report, the reconciliation step explains why the impression count in the platform differs from the exposed-user count the visit partner reports. The platform counts every delivered impression; the visit partner counts only the subset of users it could match to a device ID and a store visit.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running awareness campaigns with a brand lift partner, the platform's reach estimate and the partner's exposed-respondent count will differ because the two use different methodologies. The reconciliation step notes whether the partner's exposed count is within a reasonable fraction of the platform's reach estimate, and flags it if the gap is unusually large.

investigate.intro: For each campaign with an active third-party measurement partner, confirm whether a reconciliation step took place and whether the discrepancy between partner and platform numbers falls within the expected range for the measurement type.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Pull the campaign's final platform report from The Trade Desk, noting the impression count, reach estimate, and any outcome metrics the platform tracks.
Retrieve the measurement partner's final report for the same campaign flight.

Compare the two: note the impression-to-reach ratio from the platform, the exposed-user count from the partner, and the percentage difference between the platform's reach estimate and the partner's exposed count.

Determine whether the gap is within the expected range for the measurement methodology. A brand lift partner counting survey respondents will show a much smaller number than the platform's impression count; that is expected. A reach partner whose deduplicated count exceeds the platform's unique reach estimate is unexpected and warrants investigation.
Document the comparison. Note whether the gap is explained by methodology, or whether it points to a setup issue.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each campaign with a third-party measurement partner active, whether a reconciliation step was completed and whether the discrepancy between partner and platform numbers falls within the expected range. A campaign with a documented reconciliation and a gap explained by methodology passes. A campaign with no reconciliation document, or a gap that falls outside the expected range with no explanation, is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the nature of the discrepancy.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the reconciliation status and the gap between partner and platform numbers for each campaign with a third-party partner active above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that campaigns on {{site_name}} running with third-party measurement partners are not being reconciled against The Trade Desk's platform reporting. The two sources will not produce identical numbers because they measure different dimensions of the campaign using different methodologies, and that is expected. What is not in place is a step that explains the relationship between the two sets of numbers and flags any gap that falls outside the expected variance. {{voice_recommend}} establishing a reconciliation step as part of campaign wrap-up: pull the platform report and the partner report side by side, document the expected gap for the measurement type, and flag any discrepancy that exceeds the expected range for investigation.
